title: Anticipatory Grief: Grieving Before a Loss Happens
summary: It's possible, and common, to grieve deeply for someone who hasn't died yet — this experience has a name and is a normal part of the process.
subcategory: Anticipatory Grief and Approaching End of Life
journeyStage: Anticipatory Grief and Approaching End of Life
author: HarborCare Circle Editorial
seoTitle: Anticipatory Grief: Grieving Before a Loss Happens | HarborCare Circle
metaDescription: It's possible, and common, to grieve deeply for someone who hasn't died yet — this experience has a name and is a normal part of the process.
whatYouNeedToKnow: Anticipatory grief describes mourning that begins before an actual death, often during a serious illness or decline.
It can include the same range of emotions as grief after a death — sadness, anger, relief, guilt.
It doesn't mean you love the person any less, or that you're giving up on them.

paragraphs: Anticipatory grief can begin the moment a serious or terminal diagnosis is understood, as families start to process an anticipated loss while the person is still alive — this is a real and recognized form of grief, not a sign of premature detachment.

It's common to feel a confusing mix of emotions during this time, including moments of relief alongside sadness, especially amid prolonged illness — this mix doesn't reflect badly on your love for the person; it reflects the genuine complexity of the experience.

nextSteps: Recognize that whatever you're feeling right now is a valid part of this experience.
Talk to a grief counselor or support group specifically for anticipatory grief, if it would help.
Give yourself the same compassion you'd offer a friend in this position.
disclaimer: If you're experiencing this right now, please know it's a recognized, real form of grief, and support is available — a grief counselor, hospice social worker, or support group can help.

sources.name: Providing Care and Comfort at the End of Life
